column4: Question
column6: 1 - Lagging
column7: 2 - Developing
column8: 3 - Proficient
column9: 4 - Exemplary

column4: How consistently does the backend team deliver API features and updates on predictable schedules?
column6: API releases are unpredictable or frequently delayed; deployment timelines lack visibility. Team misses sprint commitments regularly.
column7: API releases occur but timing is inconsistent; some visibility into deployment schedules exists. Most sprints are met but with occasional slippage.
column8: API releases follow a predictable cadence aligned with sprint commitments; deployment schedules are transparent. Release windows are met consistently with clear communication of delays.
column9: API delivery cadence is highly predictable and optimized; deployment automation enables frequent releases. The team proactively communicates roadmap and timelines; zero missed release windows.

column4: To what extent are APIs meeting reliability targets while maintaining code quality standards?
column6: APIs experience frequent outages or errors (uptime <95%); code quality issues are common and unreviewed. Technical debt accumulates rapidly.
column7: API uptime is around 95-97%; code quality is inconsistent. Some reviews occur but standards are not uniformly enforced.
column8: API uptime consistently meets 99%+ targets; code adheres to documented standards. Peer review is thorough and catches issues before production.
column9: APIs exceed 99.5%+ uptime with zero critical production regressions; code is maintainable and well-tested. Quality reviews are automated and comprehensive.

column4: Is the backend team's capacity allocated effectively across API development and support responsibilities?
column6: Team is either overloaded or underutilized; workload distribution is unfair or unplanned. Key individuals carry excessive load.
column7: Workload is generally distributed but with occasional bottlenecks; some team members carry heavier loads. Allocation planning exists but lacks flexibility.
column8: Team capacity is well-balanced across projects and on-call rotations; allocation decisions are documented. Individuals have sustainable workloads with clear ownership.
column9: Workload distribution is optimized dynamically; team members rotate responsibilities to prevent burnout. Allocation enables skill development while maintaining delivery.

column4: How effectively does the backend team use CI/CD pipelines and innovate deployment processes?
column6: CI/CD pipelines are minimal or non-existent; deployments are manual and error-prone. No process improvement initiatives.
column7: CI/CD pipelines exist but with gaps; manual steps remain in deployment. Occasional process improvements are attempted.
column8: CI/CD pipelines are comprehensive and automated; deployments are reliable and fast. Continuous improvement is systematic and tracked.
column9: CI/CD is fully automated with canary/blue-green deployments; team constantly innovates pipeline tooling. Deployment failure rate is <1%.

column4: Does the backend team ensure APIs are production-ready before release with appropriate gatekeeping controls?
column6: APIs are released without readiness checks; gatekeeping is absent or ineffective. Production issues are common.
column7: Release readiness checklist exists but is inconsistently applied. Some gatekeeping occurs but gaps remain.
column8: Pre-release checklist is rigorous and consistently applied; performance and security gates are automated. Dependencies are validated.
column9: Release gates are comprehensive and automated; performance baselines are enforced. Zero production rollbacks due to release process failures.

column4: How transparent is the backend team in communicating API status and incidents to Frontend/Mobile/Hardware teams?
column6: Incident and status information is not shared; stakeholders are left unaware of API issues. Communication is reactive and incomplete.
column7: API status updates occur but inconsistently; incident communication is delayed. Stakeholders sometimes lack full context.
column8: API status dashboard is maintained; incidents are communicated proactively with context and ETA. Regular health reports are shared.
column9: API status is real-time and publicly accessible; incident communication includes root cause and prevention measures. Stakeholder feedback shapes prioritization.

column4: How well does the backend team respond to incidents and drive resolution through root cause analysis?
column6: Incident response is slow or disorganized; root cause analysis is absent or superficial. Incidents reoccur frequently.
column7: Incidents are addressed but response time is variable; RCA is conducted but action items are not always tracked.
column8: Incident response SLAs are met; RCA is thorough and drives prevention measures. Follow-up actions are completed.
column9: Incident response is <15min; RCA is comprehensive with timeline analysis. Preventive measures prevent recurrence; post-incident reviews drive systemic improvements.

column4: Are API specifications complete
column6: and validated before development begins?
column7: High
column8: API specs are missing or incomplete; no review process exists. Development begins without clarity.
column9: API specs exist but are incomplete in places; minimal review occurs before development. Standards are unclear.
column10: API specs are comprehensive (OpenAPI/GraphQL schema); peer review is required before development. Standards are documented.
column11: API specs are auto-validated against standards; breaking changes are flagged. Specs are versioned and tracked as source of truth.

column4: How effectively does the backend head identify and remove blockers that impede team progress?
column6: Blockers accumulate without resolution; team is frequently stalled. Leadership is unaware of impediments.
column7: Blockers are identified but removal is slow; some team members wait for unblocking. Escalation path is unclear.
column8: Blockers are identified and removed within 48 hours; team updates are weekly. Cross-team coordination is effective.
column9: Blockers are proactively anticipated and removed same-day; team flow is optimized. Escalation paths are clear and ownership is assigned.

column4: Do backend APIs and services follow a consistent architecture and maintain thorough documentation?
column6: Architecture is inconsistent or ad-hoc; documentation is absent or outdated. Tech choices lack justification.
column7: Architecture patterns exist but are not uniformly applied; documentation is partial. Standards are being defined.
column8: Architecture is well-documented with design decision records; patterns are consistently applied. Integration guides are current.
column9: Architecture is enforced via code review and automated checks; documentation is auto-generated from specs. Every API has runbooks.

column4: How rigorous is the code review process and does the team use it for knowledge sharing and mentorship?
column6: Code review is minimal or rubber-stamped; junior engineers receive little mentorship. Bugs slip through.
column7: Code review occurs but feedback is surface-level; mentorship is informal. Some knowledge sharing happens.
column8: Code review is thorough with actionable feedback; junior engineers are mentored via reviews. Design discussions occur.
column9: Code review includes architecture validation; mentorship is structured. Reviewers pair with authors on complex changes; knowledge spreads organically.

column4: How effectively does the backend team track and address technical debt?
column6: Technical debt is ignored or untracked; codebase becomes increasingly brittle. Refactoring never happens.
column7: Technical debt exists and is acknowledged but prioritization is ad-hoc. Some effort is made to reduce it.
column8: Technical debt is tracked with impact scores; 20-30% of sprint capacity is allocated to debt reduction.
column9: Technical debt is quantified and tied to business impact; sprints reserve 30%+ capacity. Automated tools flag debt; deprecation is planned.

column4: Does the backend team actively share knowledge and evolve technical skills?
column6: Knowledge is siloed; junior engineers struggle to learn. No formal knowledge-sharing activities.
column7: Knowledge is shared informally; learning happens ad-hoc. Some pairing or mentoring occurs.
column8: Team has regular tech talks and design reviews; documentation enables self-service learning. Skill gaps are identified and addressed.
column9: Team has formalized knowledge-sharing (wiki
column11: open code sessions). Skill gaps drive learning plans; engineers rotate between API/database/infrastructure areas.

column4: Is the backend team environment one where engineers feel safe to speak up
column6: and take risks?
column7: High
column8: Team climate is tense or hierarchical; speaking up is discouraged. Blame is common when errors occur.
column9: Team has open communication but some fear of judgment remains. Risk-taking is cautiously supported.
column10: Team is psychologically safe; mistakes are treated as learning opportunities. Experimentation is encouraged within guardrails.
column11: Team actively invites dissent and diverse perspectives; failure is celebrated as learning. Blame-free culture drives continuous improvement.

column4: What is the backend team's retention rate and overall engagement level?
column6: Turnover is high (>20% annually); exit interviews cite poor leadership or culture. Engagement is low.
column7: Turnover is moderate (15-20%); some disengagement is evident. Retention efforts are reactive.
column8: Turnover is low (<10% annually); engagement scores are above company average. Career paths are clear.
column9: Turnover is <5%; engagement is consistently high. Team members actively recruit peers; zero involuntary departures.

column4: Does the backend team systematically capture and share API/infrastructure knowledge with Frontend/Mobile/Hardware teams?
column6: Knowledge is rarely shared outside backend; other teams have difficulty integrating APIs. Documentation is lacking.
column7: Knowledge is shared reactively; API documentation is available but incomplete. Ad-hoc support to other teams.
column8: Backend proactively documents APIs and hosts integration sessions; cross-team knowledge-sharing is systematic. Runbooks are accessible.
column9: Backend hosts quarterly API workshops; integration support is embedded. Knowledge base is auto-generated from specs and updated in real-time.

column4: Does the backend head clearly articulate the backend vision and how it connects to company goals and team purpose?
column6: Backend vision is unclear or disconnected from company strategy. Team is unclear on purpose.
column7: Vision exists but is not consistently communicated. Team members have varying understanding of purpose.
column8: Backend vision is clear and tied to company OKRs; team understands how API work impacts product.
column9: Vision is compelling and regularly reinforced; team sees direct connection between their work and customer outcomes.

column4: How effectively does the backend head identify
column6: and measure impact of strategic initiatives?
column7: Medium
column8: Strategic initiatives are absent or poorly defined; impact is not measured. Work feels ad-hoc.
column9: Initiatives are defined but execution is inconsistent; impact metrics are lacking or unclear.
column10: Initiatives are clear with defined success metrics; progress is tracked quarterly. Business impact is communicated.
column11: Initiatives are data-driven with pre- and post-launch metrics; impact directly influences product roadmap decisions.

column4: Does the backend head prioritize work based on risk assessment and potential system impact?
column6: Prioritization is ad-hoc or driven by noise; high-risk areas are ignored. Outages are frequent.
column7: Prioritization is mostly reactive; some risk assessment occurs. High-risk items are addressed but timing is reactive.
column8: Prioritization is risk-based with impact scoring; high-risk APIs receive proactive hardening. Risk register is maintained.
column9: High-risk systems have redundancy and failover; risk-based prioritization prevents >90% of potential outages.

column4: How transparent is the backend head in communicating trade-offs between speed
column6: and stability?
column7: Medium
column8: Trade-offs are made unilaterally without team input; rationale is not communicated.
column9: Trade-offs are discussed but rationale is sometimes unclear. Team is consulted occasionally.
column10: Trade-offs are clearly articulated with documented rationale; team is consulted on major decisions. Decisions are reversible.
column11: Trade-offs are decided collaboratively with stakeholders; impact analysis is shared; team feels ownership of decisions.

column4: How responsive is the backend head to team needs and how well are feedback loops established?
column6: Feedback is not solicited or acted upon; team requests are ignored. Responsiveness is slow.
column7: Feedback is occasionally gathered; response to requests is variable. Some improvements result from feedback.
column8: Feedback is regularly solicited and systematically reviewed; improvements are tracked and communicated. Response time is <1 week.
column9: Feedback loops are continuous; changes driven by feedback are celebrated. Response time is <24 hours; team sees their input acted upon.

column4: How mature and effective are the backend team's automation and tooling investments?
column6: Automation is minimal; tooling is outdated or poorly integrated. Manual toil is high.
column7: Automation covers basic CI/CD; tooling is functional but has gaps. Some teams use different tools.
column8: Automation covers deployment
column9: testing
column10: and monitoring; tooling is standardized. Toil is <20% of sprint capacity.
column14: alerting); tooling is integrated. Toil is <10%; team focuses on value-add work.

column4: How effectively does the backend head identify and close skill gaps within the team?
column6: Skill gaps are not addressed; team struggles with critical technologies. Learning is discouraged.
column7: Skill gaps are identified but closure plans are informal. Some learning happens on-the-job.
column8: Skill gaps are tracked; learning plans are in place. Training budget is allocated and used.
column9: Skill gaps drive structured learning paths; certifications are supported. Rotation across API/database/infrastructure closes gaps systematically.

column4: How effectively does the backend head lead technical evolution and manage organizational change?
column6: Change is imposed top-down; resistance is not managed. Evolution is stalled.
column7: Change is communicated but adoption is slow; resistance is sometimes managed.
column8: Change is communicated with rationale; pilot programs test before rollout. Team is engaged in evolution planning.
column9: Change is co-designed with team; pilots are rapid and feedback drives decisions. Evolution is continuous and team-led.

column4: How much autonomy and decision-making authority does the backend team have?
column6: Team lacks authority; all decisions are escalated. Autonomy is minimal.
column7: Team has some autonomy but decisions are frequently overridden. Authority boundaries are unclear.
column8: Team has clear authority to make tactical decisions; strategic decisions are collaborative. Autonomy increases with experience.
column9: Team is fully empowered to own API strategy; escalations are rare. Junior engineers are gradually given more authority.

column3: Culture of Experimentation & Creative Problem Solving
column4: Does the backend team embrace experimentation and creative approaches to technical challenges?
column6: Experimentation is discouraged; "tried and true" approaches are enforced. Problem-solving is rigid.
column7: Experimentation is tolerated but not encouraged; some creative solutions emerge ad-hoc.
column8: Experimentation is encouraged; team runs small A/B tests and pilots. Failure is accepted.
column9: Team proactively experiments (feature flags
column10: shadow traffic testing); learning from experiments drives innovation. Risk is carefully managed but balanced against exploration.
